# SSM Framework

The state-space model framework is the experimental side of smckit. It
re-expresses SMC methods as explicit transition, emission, and initial-state
objects so they can be fitted with alternative optimization strategies such as
JAX-based gradient descent.

```{admonition} Best for
:class: tip
Method development, experimentation, and gradient-based alternatives to the
classic EM pipelines.
```

## What it gives you

- `SmcStateSpace` as a shared abstraction
- `PsmcSSM` as the first concrete implementation
- gradient or EM fitting routes through the same model object

## Relationship to the dual implementation philosophy

This framework is native-only today. It is not an upstream compatibility layer
and does not have an `implementation="upstream"` target of its own.

## Recommended starting workflow

```python
from smckit.ext.ssm import PsmcSSM
import smckit

data = smckit.io.read_psmcfa(smckit.io.example_path("psmc/NA12878_chr22.psmcfa"))
observations = [rec["codes"] for rec in data.uns["records"]]

model = PsmcSSM(pattern="4+5*3+4")
params = model.make_initial_params(data.uns["sum_L"], data.uns["sum_n"])
result = model.fit(observations, params, method="gradient", n_iterations=500)
```

## How to think about the workflow

This page is about an experimental modeling framework, not one top-level
`smckit.tl.*` function.

- `PsmcSSM(...)` defines the state-space model you want to fit.
- `make_initial_params(...)` creates a valid starting parameter object from the
  input summary statistics.
- `fit(...)` runs an optimizer on explicit observations and returns optimizer
  outputs rather than a standard `data.results[...]` payload.

## What the main knobs mean

| Step / argument | What it means | When to change it | Default guidance |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pattern` in `PsmcSSM(...)` | Time-parameter grouping, conceptually similar to PSMC. | Change when experimenting with different demographic flexibility. | Start with the standard PSMC-style pattern. |
| `method="gradient"` in `fit(...)` | Use gradient-based optimization instead of classic EM. | Change when comparing optimization strategies. | This is part of the point of the framework. |
| `n_iterations` in `fit(...)` | Optimization length. | Increase when the loss is still improving. | Expect to inspect optimizer history, not just the endpoint. |

## What to inspect after fitting

The returned fit object is for experimentation:

- inspect optimizer history
- inspect loss curves
- compare fits under different optimization methods or model parameterizations

Use the standard PSMC API when you want the most battle-tested public workflow
and the familiar `data.results["psmc"]` structure.

## Common confusion points

- This is not a replacement for `smckit.tl.psmc` in everyday analysis.
- The output structure is framework-oriented, not standardized like the method
  results in `data.results`.
- Experimental does not mean unusable, but it does mean you should validate
  more and assume less.

## Learn more

- [PSMC-SSM guide](../guide/psmc-ssm.md)
- [API reference](../api/ssm.rst)
- [PSMC](psmc.md)
